proofs.proof_tex:
Fix nu in M_tan and u=(u_0,u_1) with 0<J_nu(u)<infinity. By lem:arm-score-program-solution, for each arm a there is a bounded score s_a=s_a^{u_a} satisfying int s_a dnu_a=0, int y s_a dnu_a=0, int y^2 s_a dnu_a=u_a, and int s_a^2 dnu_a=J_{a,nu}(u_a). Lemma lem:linear-tilt-path-valid then constructs, for all sufficiently small |h|, arm marginals dnu_a^h=(1+h s_a)dnu_a supported on [0,1], preserving the arm first moment exactly, perturbing the second moment as m_a(h)^2=m_a^2+h u_a, and satisfying KL(nu_a^h,nu_a)=(h^2/2)J_{a,nu}(u_a)+o(h^2).

Define nu^(u,0)=nu. For h not equal to zero, choose any coupling of the two tilted arm marginals, for instance the product coupling. This coupling is supported on [0,1]^2, so bounded support is preserved. The observed likelihood, the Neyman objective V_nu, the oracle allocation pi_nu_star, and the information calculation depend only on the arm marginals. Thus h -> nu^(u,h) is a bounded-support path through nu satisfying every clause of def:local-path-handle.

Fix nu in M_tan and write p=pi_nu_star=m_1/(m_0+m_1). By ass:interior-second-moments, p lies in (0,1). By ass:arm-tangent-nondegeneracy and the definition of M_tan, r_{a,nu}>0 for a=0,1. Lemma lem:arm-score-program-solution gives J_{a,nu}(x)=x^2/r_{a,nu}. Hence J_nu(u)=p u_1^2/r_{1,nu}+(1-p)u_0^2/r_{0,nu}, a positive definite quadratic form on R^2.

The map dot_pi_nu(u) is a nonzero linear functional: for example, dot_pi_nu((0,1))=m_0/(2m_1(m_0+m_1)^2)>0. Therefore u=(0,1) has 0<J_nu(u)<infinity and dot_pi_nu(u) not equal to zero, so U_nu is nonempty.

Lemma lem:local-complexity-rayleigh gives the exact formula kappa_nu={r_{0,nu}m_1^2/m_0^3+r_{1,nu}m_0^2/m_1^3}/[4(m_0+m_1)]. Every term is finite and positive because 0<m_a<=1 under bounded outcomes and r_{a,nu}>0. Thus 0<kappa_nu<infinity.

Write S=m_0+m_1 and p=pi_nu_star=m_1/S. Then m_1=Sp and m_0=S(1-p). By lem:neyman-gap-identity, for every pi in (0,1), g_nu(pi)=S^2(pi-p)^2/[pi(1-pi)]. Substituting pi=p+delta gives g_nu(p+delta)=S^2 delta^2/[(p+delta)(1-p-delta)]. Since p is in (0,1), the denominator tends to p(1-p)=m_0m_1/S^2. Therefore g_nu(p+delta)=S^4 delta^2/(m_0m_1)+o(delta^2)=H_nu delta^2+o(delta^2).

Now take any path satisfying def:local-path-handle. Its arm second moments satisfy m_a(h)^2=m_a^2+h u_a+o(h). Since m_a>0, the square-root expansion gives m_a(h)=m_a+h u_a/(2m_a)+o(h). Differentiating p(h)=m_1(h)/(m_0(h)+m_1(h)) at h=0 yields p'(0)={m_0 u_1/(2m_1)-m_1 u_0/(2m_0)}/S^2=dot_pi_nu(u). Hence pi_{nu^(u,h)}_star=pi_nu_star+h dot_pi_nu(u)+o(h).

Fix nu in M_tan and epsilon>0. By lem:feasible-directions, U_nu is nonempty and 0<kappa_nu<infinity. Since kappa_nu is the supremum of S_nu^2 dot_pi_nu(u)^2/J_nu(u) over u in U_nu, with S_nu=m_0+m_1, there is u_epsilon in U_nu such that S_nu^2 dot_pi_nu(u_epsilon)^2/J_nu(u_epsilon)>=kappa_nu-epsilon. Because H_nu=S_nu^2/[p(1-p)] with p=pi_nu_star and p(1-p)<=1, the same direction also satisfies H_nu dot_pi_nu(u_epsilon)^2/J_nu(u_epsilon)>=kappa_nu-epsilon.

By lem:linear-tilt-path-valid, this u_epsilon has a bounded-support linear-tilt path h -> nu^(u_epsilon,h) satisfying the path handle used in lem:path-existence. Applying the radius-monotone form of lem:local-neighborhood-cumulative-risk to nu and u_epsilon gives bar_eta>0 such that every eta in (0,bar_eta] has a threshold T_0(eta) with

sup_{|h|<=eta} mathfrak_R_T(Alg,nu^(u_epsilon,h)) >= c_0 S_nu^2 dot_pi_nu(u_epsilon)^2 J_nu(u_epsilon)^{-1} log T

for every predictable Bernoulli adaptive design Alg and every T>=T_0(eta). Choose any eta in (0,bar_eta]. The choice of u_epsilon then yields sup_{|h|<=eta} mathfrak_R_T(Alg,nu^(u_epsilon,h)) >= c_0 (kappa_nu-epsilon) log T. This is the asserted instance lower bound, with a universal positive numerical constant.

This is an instance-level superpopulation Horvitz-Thompson cumulative variance-regret converse. It is distinct from design-based AIPW adaptive-allocation upper-bound work such as Chen-Ge-Qian-Harshaw (2025), and from treatment-choice or simple-regret lower bounds: the loss controlled here is the accumulated Neyman variance inefficiency of the adaptive Horvitz-Thompson ATE estimator. The contribution is the first nontrivial minimax lower bound for this named superpopulation Horvitz-Thompson objective and it matches the published O(log T) rate scale, not a constant-sharp two-sided minimax value.

Let 0<underline_m<overline_m<=1 and underline_r>0, and assume the strict interior subclass M^circ is nonempty. For every nu in M^circ, lem:feasible-directions gives kappa_nu>0. Also kappa_nu is finite, and on the band it is uniformly bounded above by lem:local-complexity-rayleigh because r_{a,nu}<=int y^4 dnu_a<=1 and m_a>=underline_m. Hence K_circ:=sup_{nu in M^circ} kappa_nu is a finite positive number.

Fix alpha in (0,K_circ/4). Choose nu_alpha in M^circ with kappa_{nu_alpha}>=K_circ-alpha. Write S_alpha=m_0(nu_alpha)+m_1(nu_alpha). By the definition of kappa and lem:feasible-directions, choose u_alpha in U_{nu_alpha} such that S_alpha^2 dot_pi_{nu_alpha}(u_alpha)^2/J_{nu_alpha}(u_alpha)>=kappa_{nu_alpha}-alpha>=K_circ-2alpha. Lemma lem:linear-tilt-path-valid gives the corresponding bounded linear-tilt path h -> nu_alpha^(u_alpha,h).

Since nu_alpha is strict in the band and the witness path is a fixed finite-information linear tilt, lem:band-continuity-for-linear-tilts gives eta_1>0 such that nu_alpha^(u_alpha,h) lies in M(underline_m,overline_m,underline_r) for every |h|<=eta_1. The radius-monotone form of lem:local-neighborhood-cumulative-risk gives bar_eta>0 such that every eta in (0,bar_eta] is admissible for the same logarithmic lower bound, with T_0 allowed to depend on eta. Set eta=min(eta_1,bar_eta)/2. Then the whole local neighborhood {|h|<=eta} is contained in the closed regular band, and for every Alg and all T>=T_0(eta),

sup_{nu in M(underline_m,overline_m,underline_r)} mathfrak_R_T(Alg,nu) >= sup_{|h|<=eta} mathfrak_R_T(Alg,nu_alpha^(u_alpha,h)) >= c_0 (K_circ-2alpha) log T.

Taking the infimum over Alg preserves the inequality. Finally choose alpha=K_circ/4. Then the lower bound holds with c(underline_m,overline_m,underline_r)=(c_0/2)K_circ, up to the fixed numerical factor in the universal constant.

This theorem lower-bounds regret using sup_{nu in M^circ} kappa_nu on the strict interior subclass, not the closed-band frontier K. It is the first nontrivial minimax lower bound for the named superpopulation Horvitz-Thompson cumulative Neyman-regret objective. It establishes the universal minimax rate Omega(log T) on the tangent-regular subclass and matches the published O(log T) upper-rate scale of Neopane et al. and Noarov et al. The interior moment/overlap band and tangent-strength Fisher-information lower bound are standard fixed-radius regularity conditions; their radii affect only the frontier constant, not the logarithmic rate. The result is not a constant-sharp two-sided minimax theorem; the remaining open object is a constant-sharp minimax upper bound matching the lower-bound frontier.

If m_0=m_1, then def:oracle-allocation gives pi_nu_star=m_1/(m_0+m_1)=1/2. For the fixed design pi_t=1/2 at every t, each summand in def:cumulative-regret is g_nu(1/2)=V_nu(1/2)-V_nu(pi_nu_star)=0. Hence mathfrak_R_T(Alg,nu)=0.

It remains to exhibit a balanced law with positive local complexity. Let mu be any probability law on [0,1] assigning positive mass to 0, 1/2, and 1. Let nu be the product coupling with both arm marginals equal to mu. Then supp(nu) is contained in [0,1]^2, the two root second moments are equal and positive, and y^2 is not affine in y on the support {0,1/2,1}. Thus r_{0,nu}=r_{1,nu}>0, so nu belongs to M_tan. Lemma lem:feasible-directions then gives kappa_nu>0.

For any nu in the regular band, lem:local-complexity-rayleigh gives kappa_nu={r_{0,nu}m_1^2/m_0^3+r_{1,nu}m_0^2/m_1^3}/[4(m_0+m_1)]. Lemma lem:rho-envelope-closed-form says that, among all [0,1]-valued arm laws with root second moment v, the largest possible tangent strength r is rho(v), that rho is continuous on (0,1] with rho(1)=0, and that this value is attained by the three-point law specified in def:frontier-duality-handle. Consequently an arm with root second moment v can satisfy the regular-band tangent condition r>=underline_r if and only if rho(v)>=underline_r. This is exactly the feasible scalar set F(underline_m,overline_m,underline_r).

Because the coefficients multiplying r_{0,nu} and r_{1,nu} in the Rayleigh formula are positive, the supremum of kappa_nu over the regular band is obtained by maximizing each armwise r_a at fixed m_a. Therefore K(underline_m,overline_m,underline_r)=sup_{m_0,m_1 in F(underline_m,overline_m,underline_r)} Phi(m_0,m_1), where Phi is the function in def:frontier-duality-handle.

If F is empty, no arm marginal in the moment band can have r>=underline_r, so M(underline_m,overline_m,underline_r) is empty and no attainment law is asserted. If F is nonempty, F is closed in the compact interval [underline_m,overline_m] because rho is continuous, hence F^2 is compact. Since underline_m>0, Phi is continuous on F^2. The supremum is therefore a maximum. At a maximizer (m_0,m_1), choose for each arm the three-point extremal law from lem:rho-envelope-closed-form and couple the arms arbitrarily, for instance by product coupling. The resulting bounded-support nu lies in the regular band and attains the displayed value of K.

This solves the class-level local lower-bound frontier K over the closed regular band. Separately, thm:global-log-rate converts the strict-interior sup of kappa_nu into a matching-rate Omega(log T) minimax lower bound. These are aligned but not identical objects: K is the closed-band local frontier, while thm:global-log-rate uses sup_{nu in M^circ} kappa_nu to keep the local tilt inside the closed band. The constant-sharp upper bound matching K up to the universal multiplicative constant remains a separate question not implied by this lower-bound frontier alone.

added_lemmas.statement: For nu in M_tan and a in {0,1}, let e_a be the L2(nu_a) residual of y^2 after projection on span{1,y}. Then r_{a,nu}=int e_a^2 dnu_a>0. For every x in R, J_{a,nu}(x)=x^2/r_{a,nu}; when x is nonzero the minimizer is s_a^x=x e_a/r_{a,nu}, and for x=0 the minimizer is s_a^0=0. These scores are bounded on [0,1].
added_lemmas.proof_tex: Work in the Hilbert space L2(nu_a). Let L=span{1,y}, a finite-dimensional closed subspace. By def:arm-tangent-strengths, r_{a,nu}=inf_{ell in L} ||y^2-ell||_2^2. Since nu lies in M_tan, this value is positive. Let e_a=y^2-Proj_L(y^2). Then e_a is orthogonal to 1 and y, and ||e_a||_2^2=r_{a,nu}. If s is feasible for J_{a,nu}(x), then int s dnu_a=0 and int y s dnu_a=0, so x=int y^2 s dnu_a=int e_a s dnu_a. Cauchy-Schwarz gives x^2<=||s||_2^2 r_{a,nu}, hence every feasible score has cost at least x^2/r_{a,nu}. The score s_a^x=x e_a/r_{a,nu} satisfies the mass and first-moment constraints, has int y^2 s_a^x dnu_a=x, and has squared norm x^2/r_{a,nu}; for x=0 this is the zero score. Thus J_{a,nu}(x)=x^2/r_{a,nu}. Since y is supported on [0,1] and e_a is a quadratic polynomial minus an affine polynomial with finite coefficients, e_a and s_a^x are bounded on [0,1].

added_lemmas.statement: For nu in M_tan and every u with 0<J_nu(u)<infinity, the scores from lem:arm-score-program-solution define a linear-tilt path dnu_a^h=(1+h s_a^{u_a})dnu_a for small |h|. This path has bounded support, preserves each arm mean exactly, perturbs the arm second moment as m_a(h)^2=m_a^2+h u_a, and satisfies KL(nu_a^h,nu_a)=(h^2/2)J_{a,nu}(u_a)+o(h^2).
added_lemmas.proof_tex: Let s_a=s_a^{u_a} be the bounded score supplied by lem:arm-score-program-solution. Choose eta>0 such that |h| ||s_a||_infty<=1/2 for both arms whenever |h|<=eta. Then 1+h s_a is nonnegative and integrates to one because int s_a dnu_a=0, so dnu_a^h=(1+h s_a)dnu_a is a probability law supported on [0,1]. Its first moment is int y dnu_a+h int y s_a dnu_a=int y dnu_a. Its second moment is int y^2 dnu_a+h int y^2 s_a dnu_a=m_a^2+h u_a. Boundedness of s_a gives the uniform expansion (1+h s_a)log(1+h s_a)=h s_a+(h^2/2)s_a^2+o(h^2), and the linear term integrates to zero. Therefore KL(nu_a^h,nu_a)=(h^2/2)int s_a^2 dnu_a+o(h^2)=(h^2/2)J_{a,nu}(u_a)+o(h^2).

added_lemmas.statement: For any law with m_0,m_1>0, let S=m_0+m_1 and p=pi_nu_star=m_1/S. Then for every pi in (0,1), V_nu(pi)-V_nu(p)=S^2(pi-p)^2/[pi(1-pi)].
added_lemmas.proof_tex: Since p=m_1/S, m_1=Sp and m_0=S(1-p). Hence V_nu(pi)=S^2 p^2/pi+S^2(1-p)^2/(1-pi), while V_nu(p)=S^2 p+S^2(1-p)=S^2. Thus V_nu(pi)-V_nu(p)=S^2{p^2/pi+(1-p)^2/(1-pi)-1}. Putting the terms over pi(1-pi), the numerator is p^2(1-pi)+(1-p)^2 pi-pi(1-pi)=p^2-2p pi+pi^2=(pi-p)^2. This proves the identity.

added_lemmas.statement: Let h have a continuously differentiable prior density q on an interval [a,b], with q(a)=q(b)=0 and I_q=int (q'(h)^2/q(h))dh<infinity. Let Z have law P_h differentiable in quadratic mean with score S_h and Fisher information I(h)=E_h S_h^2. For any square-integrable estimator delta(Z) of a differentiable scalar psi(h), E_q E_h[(delta(Z)-psi(h))^2] >= (int psi'(h)q(h)dh)^2 / (I_q + int I(h)q(h)dh). The same bound holds when Z is the history of a predictable sequential experiment, with I(h) equal to the Fisher information of the joint likelihood through that history.
added_lemmas.proof_tex: Let E denote expectation under the joint density q(h)dP_h(z). The joint score for h is S_h(Z)+q'(h)/q(h). Since delta(Z) does not depend on h and q vanishes at the endpoints, integration by parts gives E[(delta(Z)-psi(h))(S_h(Z)+q'(h)/q(h))]=int psi'(h)q(h)dh. Cauchy-Schwarz bounds the square of this identity by E[(delta-psi)^2] E[(S_h+q'/q)^2]. The cross term is zero because E_h S_h=0 for each h, so the second factor is int I(h)q(h)dh+I_q. This proves the displayed inequality. In a predictable sequential experiment, the likelihood factorizes into conditional likelihoods. Differentiating the product gives an additive martingale score, and conditional orthogonality makes the Fisher information the sum of the conditional Fisher informations through the observed history. Applying the same integration-by-parts argument to this joint likelihood proves the sequential version.

added_lemmas.statement: Fix nu in M_tan and u in U_nu. For the linear-tilt path h -> nu^(u,h), with S=m_0+m_1, there exists bar_eta>0 such that for every eta in (0,bar_eta] there is T_0(eta)<infinity such that every Alg in A_T satisfies sup_{|h|<=eta} mathfrak_R_T(Alg,nu^(u,h)) >= c_0 S^2 dot_pi_nu(u)^2 J_nu(u)^{-1} log T for all T>=T_0(eta), where c_0>0 is a universal numerical constant.
added_lemmas.proof_tex:
Write p_h=pi_{nu^(u,h)}_star, S_h=m_0(h)+m_1(h), J=J_nu(u), d=dot_pi_nu(u), and S=m_0+m_1. Since u lies in U_nu, J>0 and d is nonzero. By prop:quadratic-loss-expansion and the exact moment formulas for the linear tilt, there is bar_eta>0 such that for every |h|<=bar_eta, the following four bounds hold simultaneously: p'_h has the same sign as d and |p'_h|>=|d|/2; S_h^2>=S^2/2; p_h stays in a compact subinterval of (0,1); and |p_h I_1(h)+(1-p_h)I_0(h)-J|<=J/4, where I_a(h)=int s_a(y)^2/(1+h s_a(y)) dnu_a(y) is the armwise Fisher information of the tilted marginal. By shrinking bar_eta if necessary, 1+h s_a is bounded away from zero for both arms on this interval. Lemma lem:neyman-gap-identity gives, for every pi in (0,1),

g_{nu^(u,h)}(pi)=S_h^2(pi-p_h)^2/[pi(1-pi)].

Since pi(1-pi)<=1/4, we have g_{nu^(u,h)}(pi)>=4S_h^2(pi-p_h)^2>=2S^2(pi-p_h)^2 for every |h|<=bar_eta.

Now fix any eta in (0,bar_eta] and any adaptive design Alg. Choose a C^1 prior density q_eta on [-eta,eta] with q_eta(-eta)=q_eta(eta)=0 and prior Fisher information I_q=int (q_eta')^2/q_eta=C_q eta^{-2}. Define

b_t=int E_h[(pi_t-p_h)^2]q_eta(h)dh,    B_T=sum_{t=1}^T b_t,

and let R_T^B=int E_h sum_{t=1}^T g_{nu^(u,h)}(pi_t) q_eta(h)dh be the Bayes-average cumulative regret. The quadratic inequality above gives R_T^B>=2S^2 B_T.

For the history before time t, predictability and Bernoulli randomization give the conditional Fisher information under h as

sum_{s<t} E_h[pi_s I_1(h)+(1-pi_s)I_0(h)].

Decompose the summand as p_h I_1(h)+(1-p_h)I_0(h)+(pi_s-p_h)(I_1(h)-I_0(h)). The first term is at most 5J/4 on [-eta,eta]. The second term is bounded in absolute value by L |pi_s-p_h| for a finite path constant L, because the scores are bounded and 1+h s_a is bounded away from zero. Averaging over q_eta and applying Cauchy-Schwarz gives integrated Fisher information before t at most (5J/4)t+L sqrt(t B_{t-1}). Applying lem:bayes-cramer-rao-predictable to the estimator delta=pi_t of psi(h)=p_h based on H_{t-1}, and using int |p'_h| q_eta(h)dh>=|d|/2 with constant sign, yields

b_t >= (d^2/4)/(C_q eta^{-2}+(5J/4)t+L sqrt(t B_{t-1})).

Let A>0 be a sufficiently small numerical constant. If R_T^B>=A S^2 d^2 J^{-1} log T, then the desired lower bound follows because sup_{|h|<=eta} mathfrak_R_T(Alg,nu^(u,h)) is at least the Bayes average. Otherwise B_T<=A d^2 J^{-1}log T/2. Set t_*=ceil(C_* log T), where C_* is a finite constant chosen for the fixed local path so that the feedback term satisfies L sqrt(t A d^2 J^{-1}log T/2) <= (J/2)t for all t in [t_*,T]. Since eta is fixed, choose T_0(eta) large enough that also C_q eta^{-2}<=(J/4)t for every t>=t_* and T>=T_0(eta). Hence, for T>=T_0(eta) and all t in [t_*,T], the denominator in the lower bound for b_t is at most 2Jt, and therefore b_t>=d^2/(8Jt).

Consequently

B_T>=sum_{t=t_*}^T d^2/(8Jt)=(d^2/(8J)){log T-log(C_* log T)+O(1)}.

The threshold is explicit, t_*=Theta(log T), and log(C_* log T)=o(log T). Increasing T_0(eta) if necessary gives B_T>=c d^2 J^{-1} log T for a universal c>0. Multiplying by 2S^2 gives R_T^B>=c_0 S^2 d^2 J^{-1} log T after reducing c_0 if necessary. Finally sup_{|h|<=eta} mathfrak_R_T(Alg,nu^(u,h))>=R_T^B. Grouping the harmonic tail into dyadic blocks [2^k,2^{k+1}) is the equivalent epoched multi-scale view: each epoch beyond t_* contributes a constant amount, while all information from earlier epochs is already charged in the Fisher-information denominator. This proves the stated lower bound for every eta in (0,bar_eta], with T_0 allowed to depend on eta.

added_lemmas.statement: Let nu lie in the strict interior subclass M^circ for fixed underline_m, overline_m, underline_r. For every fixed linear-tilt direction u with finite J_nu(u), there is eta>0 such that nu^(u,h) lies in M(underline_m,overline_m,underline_r) for all |h|<=eta.
added_lemmas.proof_tex: Along the linear-tilt path, every arm moment up to order four is a continuous function of h, because it is an integral of a bounded polynomial against (1+h s_a)dnu_a. In particular m_a(h)^2=m_a^2+h u_a is continuous, and m_a(h) is continuous for small h because m_a>0. The tangent strength r_{a,nu^(u,h)} is the minimum over b=(b_0,b_1) of int (y^2-b_0-b_1y)^2 dnu_a^h. At h=0 this minimum is positive, so the Gram matrix of (1,y) is nonsingular; by continuity of moments it remains nonsingular for small h, and the projection coefficients and minimized residual are continuous functions of moments through order four. Since nu is strict interior, m_a(0) has positive distance from underline_m and overline_m, and r_{a,nu} has positive distance above underline_r. Continuity gives eta>0 such that underline_m<=m_a(h)<=overline_m and r_{a,nu^(u,h)}>=underline_r for both arms and all |h|<=eta. Bounded support is preserved by the tilt, so nu^(u,h) belongs to M(underline_m,overline_m,underline_r).

added_lemmas.statement: For every nu in M_tan, with S=m_0+m_1, p=m_1/S, and r_a=r_{a,nu}, J_nu(u)=p u_1^2/r_1+(1-p)u_0^2/r_0 and kappa_nu={r_0 m_1^2/m_0^3+r_1 m_0^2/m_1^3}/[4S].
added_lemmas.proof_tex:
The expression for J_nu follows immediately from lem:arm-score-program-solution and def:local-information. Thus J_nu is the positive definite quadratic form p u_1^2/r_1+(1-p)u_0^2/r_0. Also dot_pi_nu(u)=[(m_0/m_1)u_1-(m_1/m_0)u_0]/(2S^2).

By the Rayleigh quotient identity for a diagonal positive quadratic form, sup_{u not equal 0} [(alpha u_1+beta u_0)^2]/[a u_1^2+b u_0^2]=alpha^2/a+beta^2/b for a,b>0. Here alpha=m_0/m_1, beta=-m_1/m_0, a=p/r_1=m_1/(S r_1), and b=(1-p)/r_0=m_0/(S r_0). Therefore sup_u S^2 dot_pi_nu(u)^2/J_nu(u)=1/(4S^2){(m_0/m_1)^2 S r_1/m_1+(m_1/m_0)^2 S r_0/m_0}. Simplifying gives kappa_nu={r_0 m_1^2/m_0^3+r_1 m_0^2/m_1^3}/[4S].

added_lemmas.statement: For v in (0,1), let q=v^2. Among all laws mu on [0,1] with int y^2 dmu=q, the supremum of r(mu)=inf_{b_0,b_1} int (y^2-b_0-b_1 y)^2 dmu is rho(v)=((mu_v-q)(q-mu_v^2))/(4mu_v(1-mu_v)), where mu_v in (q,v) is the unique root of mu^4-2mu^3+2qmu^2-2q^2mu+q^2=0. The supremum is attained by the three-point law on {0,x_v,1} with the weights displayed in def:frontier-duality-handle; rho extends continuously to v=1 by rho(1)=0.
added_lemmas.proof_tex:
Let Y be [0,1]-valued, let mu=E Y, q=E Y^2, a=E Y^3, and b=E Y^4. Necessarily q<=mu<=sqrt(q). If q is 0 or 1 the law is degenerate at 0 or 1 and r=0; hence take q in (0,1) and mu in (q,sqrt(q)). The residual variance after projecting Y^2 on span{1,Y} is r=(b-q^2)-(a-mu q)^2/(q-mu^2).

For fixed mu and q, the Hausdorff localizing inequality int Y(1-Y)(c_0+c_1Y)^2 dP>=0 gives the positive-semidefinite matrix [[mu-q,q-a],[q-a,a-b]]. Hence b<=a-(q-a)^2/(mu-q). Substituting this upper bound into the display for r gives r<=a-(q-a)^2/(mu-q)-q^2-(a-mu q)^2/(q-mu^2). The right side is a concave quadratic function of a. Its maximizer is a_*=(mu^3-mu^2 q+2mu q^2-mu q-q^2)/(2mu(mu-1)), and the maximum value is R(mu,q)=((mu-q)(q-mu^2))/(4mu(1-mu)).

The bound is sharp. Let x=(mu^2-2mu q+q)/(2mu(1-mu)) and assign masses p_x=(mu-q)/(x(1-x)), p_1=(q-x mu)/(1-x), and p_0=(x+q-(1+x)mu)/x to x,1,0. For mu in (q,sqrt(q)), these quantities are nonnegative and sum to one, and direct substitution gives E Y=mu and E Y^2=q. The same substitution gives E Y^3=a_* and saturates b=a-(q-a)^2/(mu-q), so r=R(mu,q).

It remains to maximize over mu. For q=v^2, R(mu,q) vanishes at the endpoints mu=q and mu=v and is positive inside. Differentiation gives dR/dmu=[mu^4-2mu^3+2qmu^2-2q^2mu+q^2]/[4mu^2(1-mu)^2]. The Sturm sign calculation for this quartic on (q,v) gives exactly one zero, denoted mu_v in def:frontier-duality-handle; the derivative is positive to the left of that zero and negative to the right. Hence the unique maximizer is mu_v, the envelope is rho(v)=R(mu_v,q), and the displayed three-point law with mu=mu_v attains it. The implicit-function theorem on the unique interior maximizer gives continuity of rho on (0,1). As v tends to 1, the constraint E Y^2=v^2 forces Y to converge to 1 in L2, so the residual variance r is at most Var(Y^2), which tends to zero; thus rho(v)->0=rho(1).
